sections.markdown:
# How-To: DQL for Traces

Step-by-step introduction into the possibilities of Trace DQL queries.

## What's the value of DQL access to traces?

 * **All data** is accessible via queries
   * All individual spans (units of work in a trace) are accessible
   * All span attributes are accessible (no config or setup required)
 * **Full-text searchability of all text attributes**
   * This includes, URLs, database statements, exception messages and stack traces.
 * **Powerful filtering, on any span attribute**
 * **Powerful aggregations functions, on any span attribute**
   * Response time analysis, including percentiles is possible
   * Timeseries can be extracted, with any span attribute as dimension and unlimited cardinality
 * **Full-resolution queries**
   * You can decide yourself, on which percentage of the data you want to run your query (on-read `samplingRate`)
   * Full-resolution queries on the whole retention timeframe (default: 10 days) are possible
 * **Accessible from powerful Dynatrace Apps**
   * Dashboards: Put your span queries into dashboards!
   * Workflows: Do triggered queries with notifications or writing custom metrics out of the results
   * Site Reliability Guardian: Define SLIs/SLOs from trace queries
   * Notebooks: Do deep investigations into problems or explore your data
   * Custom Apps: Write AppEngine Apps for your own custom use-cases
   * API: Integrate traces into any 3rd party application by queries via REST API

## This guide demonstrates

  1. DQL access to all spans
  1. DQL access to all attributes
  1. Full text search
  1. Service response time analysis
  1. Timeseries extraction
  1. Exception analysis
  1. Service Entities
  1. Request aggregations
  1. Trace aggregations
  1. Request attributes
  1. Logs and Traces correlation
  1. Sampling, Aggregation and Extrapolation
  1. Advanced Entity Lookups
  1. IP Addresses and networking
  1. Databases

# 1) DQL access to all spans

Spans are the building blocks of distributed traces. A single trace consists of a set of spans. Each span represents a logical unit of work within the trace.

Spans can represent HTTP request, Remote Procedure calls, Database calls, Messaging system interactions and even internal function invocations. Spans are also used by developers who directly instrument neuralgic points in their applications, to measure it's performance or failure rates.

With Traces in Grail, we provide full access to every single piece of a distributed trace, every span.

The following query shows the number of spans, per function invocation.

sections.markdown:
# 2) DQL access to all attributes

Spans are be augmented with "span attributes". These attributes are set by the instrumentation that creates the spans, be it automatic instrumentation or custom instrumentation, coded into the application.

These attributes describes the kind of operation that the span represents, with detail information about the span itself. Additionally, there is context information available about where the span originated from. This can include information about the thread, the process, the container, the pod, the host, the cloud region etc, of where this span comes from.

With Traces in Grail, any span attribute can be queried directly. No config required. The following shows a few simple examples.

### Semantic Dictionary

The semantics of all the fields will be documented in the [Semantic Dictionary](https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/platform/semantic-dictionary).

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter isNotNull(db.user)
| summarize by:{db.system, span.name, service.name, db.user, db.statement}, spans = count()

records.db.statement:
SELECT contacts.username, contacts.label, contacts.account_num, contacts.routing_num, contacts.is_external 
FROM contacts 
WHERE contacts.username = %(username_1)s
records.service.name: Contacts

FROM users 
WHERE users.username = %(username_1)s

sections.title: Example for HTTP spans

sections.markdown:
**Note:** There is a difference in field naming between Dynatrace Semantic Dictionary and older OpenTelemetry versions. In this example,
```
Old OpenTelemetry:                                http.method
New OpenTelemety & Dynatrace Semantic Dictionary: http.request.method
```

It is easy to fix such field differences in a query, with the `coalesce` statement, which takes a number of attributes as argument and picks the first one that is non-null:

```
| fieldsAdd http.request.method = coalesce(http.request.method, http.method)
```

# 3) Full text search

Every span attribute of type string is fully searchable, with all [string function available in DQL](https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/shortlink/string-functions), such as `startsWith`, `endsWith`, `contains`, or `matchesPhrase`.

The following example looks `http.route` property that contains a certain string, but does not end with another string.

sections.markdown:
# 4) Response times

The `summarize` function provides powerful numeric aggregation functions, which can be applied to the `duration` of spans. With this, average, median, or percentiles can be extracted from span data.

sections.title: Service response times with percentiles

sections.markdown: The following query looks at a specific endpoint and counts how many spans exist in certain "buckets" of durations. For every bucket, a random `trace.id` is captured. That `trace.id` serves as an "Example" of a trace that occurred within the bucket that you can drill down into.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter http.route == "/send_order_confirmation"
| summarize by:{bin(duration, 10ms)}, {spans = count(), trace = takeAny(record(start_time, trace.id))}
| fields start_time = trace[start_time], trace.id = trace[trace.id], `bin(duration, 10ms)`, spans

sections.title: Response time buckets of 10ms with "trace exemplars"

sections.markdown:
# 5) Timeseries

With the `makeTimeseries` command, a timeseries can be extracted from spans on-the-fly. Any span attribute can be used for filtering, aggregation functions, and groupings (`by` argument).

In comparison, materialized timeseries (metrics), always come with a fixed set of dimensions that need to be defined up-front. Extracting metrics from raw-data, like spans, does not have that limitation and can work with arbitrary dimensionality.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| makeTimeseries by:{http.route}, bins:250, avg = avg(duration)

sections.title: Avg, P50, P99 for specific "findByISBN" endpoint, as timeseries

sections.markdown:
# 6) Exceptions

Exceptions that happen in the context of a distributed trace are stored within individual spans as a list of `span.events`.

It's possible to query for those exceptions via `iAny` command.

It's also possible to expand and flatten the span events, so that the exception attributes appear as top level attributes. With this, it's easy to create timeseries or any other aggregation from exceptions. 

Remember, every attribute is full-text searchable and this also applies to exception messages and stack traces.

It's even possible to apply DQL `parse` command to extract structured information.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| filter iAny(span.events[][span_event.name] == "exception")
| filter iAny(NOT contains(span.events[][exception.message], "404"))
| expand span.events
| fields span.events
| fieldsFlatten span.events
| fieldsRemove span.events
| limit 1

# 7) Services Entities

Spans don't carry the service name directly as string. Instead, this information is available in Dynatrace Smartscape (Entity Model).

To enrich spans with the service name, the `entityName` is needed.

sections.markdown:
## Request aggregation

Just querying for the request root span is not enough if you want to answer a question which requires information from child spans of the request.

For this we introduce the concept of `request.id`. All spans that belong to a single request carry this ID. It can be used in a `command` to aggregated data of the request.

*Note: `request.id` are only available for OneAgent based traces, not for API Ingested Traces*

input.value:
fetch spans

// request.id is pre-requisite for this query. not always present
| filter isNotNull(request.id) 
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| summarize {
    spans = count(),
    client_spans = countIf(span.kind == "client"),
    span_events = sum(arraySize(span.events)),
    
    // from all spans in the summarized group, select the one that is the request root
    request_root = takeMin(record(
        root_detection_helper = coalesce(if(request.is_root_span, 1), 2 /* INVALID */),
        start_time, endpoint.name, duration
      ))
}, by: { trace.id, request.id }

// reset request_root to NULL if root_detection_helper is invalid       
| fieldsAdd request_root=if(request_root[root_detection_helper] < 2, request_root)
| fieldsFlatten request_root | fieldsRemove request_root.root_detection_helper, request_root

| fields 
    start_time = request_root.start_time, 
    endpoint = request_root.endpoint.name, 
    response_time = request_root.duration, 
    spans,
    client_spans, span_events,
    trace.id

| limit 100

sections.title: Aggregated requests

sections.markdown:
# 9) Trace aggregations

A full trace consists of many spans. You usually want to identify a trace by the first request it started with. The "root request".

The following query finds the root request span and additionally does aggregations over the whole trace, based on an aggregation on the `trace.id`.

input.value:
fetch spans
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| summarize {
    spans = count(),
    client_spans = countIf(span.kind == "client"),
    span_events = sum(arraySize(span.events)),

    // endpoints involved in the trace
    endpoints = toString(arrayRemoveNulls(collectDistinct(endpoint.name))),

    // hosts involved in the trace
    hosts = arrayRemoveNulls(collectDistinct(host.name)),
    
    // from all spans in the summarized group, select the one that is the first request root in the trace
    trace_root = takeMin(record(
        root_detection_helper = coalesce(if(request.is_root_span, 1), if(isNull(span.parent_id), 2), 3),
        start_time, endpoint.name, duration
      ))
}, by: { trace.id }

| fieldsFlatten trace_root | fieldsRemove trace_root.root_detection_helper, trace_root

| fields 
    start_time = trace_root.start_time, 
    endpoint = trace_root.endpoint.name, 
    response_time = trace_root.duration, 
    spans,
    client_spans, span_events,
    endpoints, hosts,
    trace.id

| sort start_time
| limit 100

sections.markdown:
# 10) Request attributes

You can also have your request attributes added on your spans. Therefore make sure that they (and also all OpenTelemetry attributes) are forwarded to Grail by selecting "**Allow all attributes**" in your **"Settings" -> "Server side monitoring" -> "Attribute capturing" -> "Preferences"**

Semantic Dictionary, will also include "request attributes" and "captured attributes". 

Request attributes are respresented as span attributes on the "request root span" (`request.is_root: true`) with the following key: `request_attribute.<name of the request attribute>`.

In case the request attribute name contains has special, you need to use backticks:
```dql
fetch spans

// no backticks required
| filter isNotNull(request_attribute.my_customer_id)

// backticks required
| filter isNotNull(`request_attribute.My Customer ID`)
```

The data-type of `request_attribute.*` depends on the request attribute config. If the config says "All values", the data-type will be an array.

If a request attribute is configured to be captured from method parameters, there are additional attributes on _the span on which the attribute has been captured_ with the following key: `captured_attribute.<name of the request attribute>`.

Note, that the data-type of `captured_attribute.*` will always be an array. This is because at capture time it is not known yet if there is only a single value or multiple values.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter isNotNull(request_attribute.TSN)
| makeTimeseries by:{request_attribute.TSN}, count()

Logs can be enriched with `trace_id` and `span_id`. OneAgent can even enrich such context into logs automatically.

With this information it is possible to join spans and logs. For example: Find traces & spans which emit specific logs.

	]

sections.markdown:
Note: Subqueries within an `in` statement do have a limit in regards to the size of the subquery result.

If the subquery result is too large, you will get a `IN_KEYWORD_TABLE_SIZE` DQL error.
From logs that contain the word "GetCartAsync", show performance of spans in which the log was emitted.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter span.id in 
	[
		fetch logs
		| filter isNotNull(span_id)
		| filter contains(content, "GetCartAsync")
		| fields toUid(span_id)
	]
| fieldsAdd name = coalesce(span.name, concat(code.namespace, ".", code.function))
| summarize by:{k8s.pod.name, name}, {count(), avg(duration), p99 = percentile(duration, 99), trace.id = takeAny(trace.id)}

sections.title: Join spans and logs

sections.markdown:
# 12) Sampling, Aggregation and Extrapolation

There are multiple situations when one span can represent multiple real operations. This can be cause by **aggregation** or by **sampling**, whereas there are multiple types of **sampling**.

## Aggregation

Certain types of operations of the same type can be aggregated into one single span. This is an optimization to reduce the amount of spans.

One example are **database spans**. If there are many database operations with the same statement, on the same database, OneAgent may choose to aggregate them. Such aggregated spans can be identified by the presence of the `aggregation.count` attribute.

## Sampling

 * **Adaptive Traffic Management (ATM)** is a rate-limiting, head-based sampling mechanism, that adaptively reacts to the rate of requests. The decision is made on the agent, at the beginning of a trace (thus "head-based").
 * **Adaptive Load Reduction (ALR)** is a server-side sampling mechanism, that is aimed to protect the backend infrastructure from being overloaded.
 * **Read Sampling** 
   * With read sampling, a user can control how much data should be read in a query, via the parameter `samplingRatio`. 
   * With a value greater than 1, only a sampled fraction of the data is read.
   * The following sampling rates are available: `1`, `10`, `100`, `1000`, `10000`, `100000`. Whereas `1` means `100%` data, and `100` means `1%` of data.
   * The actually applied read-sampling ratio can be used via `dt.system.sampling_ratio`. Even if `samplingRatio: 17`, Grail may decide to fall back to an actual sampling rate of `10`.
   * Read sampling is aware of traces. So within a specific sampling rate, you will find either all spans of a trace, or none (based on common `trace.id`).

## Extrapolation

To take all of this into account, when *counting operations*, you need to extrapolate span counts to actual operation count, by using the correct extrapolation-factor.

SaaS version 1.288 and older
```dql
// calculate multiplicity factor for every span, to for extrapolations (1.288 and older)
| fieldsAdd multiplicity = coalesce(sampling.agent_multiplicity, 1)
                         * coalesce(sampling.backend_multiplicity, 1)
                         * coalesce(aggregation.count, 1)
                         * dt.system.sampling_ratio
```

SaaS version 1.289 and newer
```dql
// calculate multiplicity factor for every span, to for extrapolations
| fieldsAdd sampling.probability = (power(2, 56) - coalesce(sampling.threshold, 0)) * power(2, -56)
| fieldsAdd sampling.multiplicity = 1/sampling.probability
| fieldsAdd multiplicity = coalesce(sampling.multiplicity, 1)
                         * coalesce(aggregation.count, 1)
                         * dt.system.sampling_ratio
```

To use the extrapolation, for example to count the number of requests, `sum()` needs to be used, instead of `count()`.
```dql
| summarize count = sum(multiplicity)
```

input.value:
fetch spans

// read only 1% of data for better read performance
, samplingRatio:100

// only request roots
| filter request.is_root_span == true

// calculate multiplicity factor for every span, to for extrapolations
| fieldsAdd sampling.probability = (power(2, 56) - coalesce(sampling.threshold, 0)) * power(2, -56)
| fieldsAdd sampling.multiplicity = 1/sampling.probability
| fieldsAdd multiplicity = coalesce(sampling.multiplicity, 1)
                         * coalesce(aggregation.count, 1)
                         * dt.system.sampling_ratio

| summarize span_count=count(), request_count_extrapolated = sum(multiplicity)

fetch spans

// read only 1% of data for better read performance
, samplingRatio:100

// only request roots
| filter request.is_root_span == true

// calculate multiplicity factor for every span, to for extrapolations
| fieldsAdd sampling.probability = (power(2, 56) - coalesce(sampling.threshold, 0)) * power(2, -56)
| fieldsAdd sampling.multiplicity = 1/sampling.probability
| fieldsAdd multiplicity = coalesce(sampling.multiplicity, 1)
                         * coalesce(aggregation.count, 1)
                         * dt.system.sampling_ratio

| summarize span_count=count(), request_count_extrapolated = sum(multiplicity)

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans, samplingRatio:100
| filter request.is_root_span == TRUE
| fieldsAdd sampling.probability = (power(2, 56) - coalesce(sampling.threshold, 0)) * power(2, -56)
| fieldsAdd sampling.multiplicity = 1 / sampling.probability
| fieldsAdd multiplicity = coalesce(sampling.multiplicity, 1) * coalesce(aggregation.count, 1) * dt.system.sampling_ratio
| summarize span_count = count(), request_count_extrapolated = sum(multiplicity)

, samplingRatio:100

# 13) Advanced entity lookups

All entities that are present on spans can be lookup up.

input.value:

| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| filter isNotNull(dt.entity.host) and isNotNull(dt.entity.service) and isNotNull(dt.entity.process_group)

            entityAttr(dt.entity.host, "bitness"),

  )
  ('", ' | '"')}{1,}:technologies"""

sections.markdown:
# 14) IP Addresses and networking

Trace data contains information about networking, for example which IP addresses a request is being made to, or IP address of a client.

The following example shows which requests all services in the environment make, including `server.address` and the IP address of that server. Note, that the field `server.resolved_ips` contains an array of IP addresses, which represent the result of a DNS lookup on the `server.address` at the time the request has been made.

grail.canonicalQuery:
fetch spans
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "production-astroshop"
| filter isNotNull(server.resolved_ips)
| summarize by:{server.address, server.port}, ips = collectDistinct(arraySort(server.resolved_ips))
| expand ips
| expand ips
| sort ips
